data.title: CACI raises 2026 guidance to $725M free cash flow and 8%-10% revenue growth as technology portfolio expands
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CACI International Inc (CACI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • John Mengucci, President and CEO, highlighted CACI's evolution beyond traditional industry labels, focusing on expanding national security capabilities: "Through deliberate actions, informed investments and flexible and opportunistic capital deployment, we have expanded our technology portfolio to nearly 60% of total revenue." Mengucci pointed to strong second quarter results, with $138 million in free cash flow, 6% revenue growth, and an 11.8% EBITDA margin. He underscored the company’s leading position in electronic warfare, which now accounts for about $2 billion in revenue, and leadership in Agile software development.
  • Mengucci announced the acquisition of ARKA as "the latest step in our technology-driven portfolio evolution and the execution of our space market strategy." He also noted, "We are raising our fiscal 2026 guidance" due to strong first half performance and increased visibility.
  • Jeffrey MacLauchlan, Executive VP and CFO, stated, "In the second quarter, we generated revenue of $2.2 billion, representing 5.7% year-over-year growth, of which 4.5% was organic." MacLauchlan cited a year-over-year EBITDA margin increase of 70 basis points and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $6.81, up 14% from the prior year. He added, "We now expect revenue to be between $9.3 billion and $9.5 billion... EBITDA margin to be in the 11.7% to 11.8% range... and free cash flow guidance to at least $725 million."

Outlook

  • Management revised guidance upwards, now expecting free cash flow of at least $725 million, revenue growth of nearly 8% to 10%, and EBITDA margin in the 11.7% to 11.8% range for fiscal 2026. Mengucci stated, "We are raising our fiscal year 2026 guidance."
  • MacLauchlan provided detailed guidance: "We now expect revenue to be between $9.3 billion and $9.5 billion... adjusted net income guidance to be between $630 million and $645 million... adjusted EPS to between $28.25 and $28.92 per share."
  • None of the projections include any benefit from the planned acquisition of ARKA.

Financial Results

  • CACI reported revenue of $2.2 billion for Q2 2026 with 5.7% year-over-year growth and 4.5% organic growth. EBITDA margin reached 11.8%, a year-over-year increase of 70 basis points.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS was $6.81, up 14% from the prior year. Free cash flow stood at $138 million for the quarter, with DSO at 57 days.
  • Backlog grew to $33 billion, up 3% year-over-year, and funded backlog increased 7% for the same period. Q2 book-to-bill was 0.65x; trailing 12-month book-to-bill stood at 1.3x.
  • Leverage at quarter end was 2.4x net debt to trailing 12-month EBITDA, with expectations to rise to 4.3x post-ARKA acquisition before returning to the low 3s within six quarters.

Q&A

  • Gavin Parsons, UBS: Asked about the impact of higher U.S. military OPTEMPO. Mengucci responded, "Today's OPTEMPO is extremely good for CACI because it requires much of what traditional companies, frankly, don't traditionally do."
  • Parsons followed up on the bid pipeline, to which MacLauchlan explained, "The length of the shutdown... put some amount of acquisition processes sort of behind the curve. We do see that filling back up."
  • Peter Arment, Baird: Inquired about a recent protest win and reconciliation funding. Mengucci shared, "JTMS protest was officially denied last week... it's a 10-year, $1.6 billion job... that's going to help us drive our fourth quarter revenue number."
  • Colin Canfield, Cantor: Asked about federal acquisition reform and ARKA scalability. Mengucci stated, "We are very well poised to support where the government is going" and described ARKA as "a leading developer and supplier of sense and sensemaking capabilities... involved in just about every critical national security mission."
  • Christopher Barbero, JPMorgan: Asked about CACI's addressable market from reconciliation bill funding. Mengucci replied, "The reconciliation dollars is a tens of billions of dollars to our addressable market... we're looking at about a $300 billion TAM."
  • Additional questions covered topics such as margin performance, government acquisition models, and the scalability of recent acquisitions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about the pace of awards, protest outcomes, scalability of acquisitions, and margin sustainability, often with a slightly positive and constructive tone, focusing on future opportunity and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing proactive investment, strategic positioning, and operational discipline. Mengucci stated, "We are confident we will continue to drive long-term shareholder value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more optimistic, underpinned by raised guidance and recent contract wins, while analysts remained focused on execution risks and operational details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • CACI raised fiscal 2026 guidance after maintaining it in Q1, reflecting increased visibility and momentum. Revenue growth targets rose from 7%-9% to nearly 8%-10%, and free cash flow guidance increased from at least $710 million to at least $725 million.
  • Margin guidance increased slightly, and management cited stronger technology portfolio contributions and successful cost controls.
  • The acquisition of ARKA was highlighted as a major new initiative, whereas the previous quarter focused on organic growth and ongoing contract wins.
  • Analysts continued to probe for risks related to government shutdowns and funding timing but shifted more attention to scalability of acquisitions and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged lingering impacts from the government shutdown, delayed program timing, and slow rebound in award decisions.
  • Leverage is set to increase to 4.3x with the ARKA acquisition, but management asserted confidence in reducing it back to the low 3s within six quarters.
  • Potential risks from changes in federal acquisition regulations and shifts from cost-plus to firm fixed price models were discussed, with management stating they are well-positioned for evolving procurement models.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of reconciliation funding, scalability of acquisitions, and possible risks for early-stage program cancellations, but management expressed confidence in mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

The company underscored its raised fiscal 2026 guidance, ongoing evolution toward a technology-driven business, and strong positioning in key growth markets like electronic warfare and Agile software. Management attributed improved outlook and resilience to deliberate investment, operational discipline, and proactive engagement with evolving customer needs, while highlighting the ARKA acquisition as a significant driver for future growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Huntington projects 13% fee revenue growth and 11% loan growth for 2026 while advancing integration of Cadence and Veritex partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (HBAN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Steinour outlined Huntington’s momentum entering its 160th year, stating, “Nearly every part of Huntington is performing at a high level, creating powerful momentum as we look to the future.” He emphasized the bank’s expansion, with a presence in 21 states and the integration of Veritex and Cadence as strategic partnerships to accelerate growth. Steinour described the operating model as a “franchise-defining competitive advantage,” emphasizing local delivery of national capabilities and a moderate to low-risk profile. He cited 2025 results of 11% revenue growth, 16% adjusted EPS growth, and 290 basis points of positive operating leverage.
  • Brantley Standridge (Senior EVP and President of Consumer & Regional Banking) explained the approach to recent partnerships: “Our approach is collaborative and transparent, designed to align around our common objectives, creating a strong foundation for long-term value creation.” Standridge highlighted the rapid integration of Veritex, completed in 187 days, and projected Cadence’s systems migration midyear 2026, with cost and revenue synergies expected to accelerate thereafter.
  • CFO Zachary Wasserman reported, “For the quarter, earnings per common share was $0.30. On an adjusted basis, excluding acquisition-related expenses and other notable items, EPS was $0.37, up 9% year-over-year.” He noted 14.4% average loan growth and 5.1% quarter-over-quarter increase in average deposits. Wasserman detailed strong performance in fee income, disciplined expense management, and robust capital generation, with tangible book value up 19% year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Management expects 2026 net interest income growth between 10% and 13%, supported by 11% to 12% loan growth and 8% to 9% deposit growth. Fee revenues are projected to rise 13% to 16%. Core expenses are expected to grow 10% to 11%, with baseline operating leverage of 150 to 200 basis points. Guidance includes expected cost synergies from Veritex fully realized by Q2 and Cadence by Q4. Cadence is forecasted to add $1.85B to $1.9B in NII and $300M in fee revenue for the year. Net charge-offs are projected between 25 and 35 basis points, with losses likely at the lower end initially.
  • The guidance language emphasized continued investment in digital capabilities and expansion in new and existing markets, noting that “our current 2026 forecast for Huntington’s stand-alone growth in NII, in assets, deposits and fees generally exceeds the growth we’ve experienced in these categories in 2025.”

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter reported EPS was $0.30, with adjusted EPS of $0.37. Average loans increased 14.4% year-over-year. Excluding Veritex, organic loan growth was $10.1B for the year. Average deposits rose 5.1% quarter-over-quarter and 8.6% year-over-year. Net interest margin for the quarter was 3.15%, up 2 basis points sequentially. Fee businesses showed broad strength, with commercial payment revenues up 8% and wealth management up 10%.
  • Operating expenses increased just $7M sequentially (excluding Veritex and one-time costs), reflecting cost discipline. Adjusted operating leverage for the year reached 290 basis points. Tangible book value grew 19% over the year, with 40% of earnings returned through dividends. Net charge-offs were 24 basis points.

Q&A

  • L. Erika Penala (UBS): Asked for clarification on expense trajectory and the layering of cost saves and investments. Zachary Wasserman answered, "Fundamentally, what we see at this point is underlying Huntington expense growth in the mid-single digits, aligned to generate 1.5 to 2 points of operating leverage.” Penala also queried the partnership approach, with Standridge responding that the model “creates a high level of certainty for the colleagues of both Veritex and Cadence” and allows for rapid realization of synergies.
  • Jon Arfstrom (RBC): Requested a tighter range for Q1 expenses. Wasserman declined to give quarterly guidance, focusing instead on annual operating leverage and marginal profitability from Cadence integration.
  • Kenneth Usdin (Bernstein Autonomous): Asked for the FY25 baseline for core expenses, to which Wasserman replied, “It’s $4.871 billion, Ken.”
  • Matthew O’Connor (Deutsche Bank): Sought details on fee contributions from recent capital market deals, with Wasserman stating, “It’s coming in sort of $80 million to $90 million of expense above revenues.”
  • Manan Gosalia (Morgan Stanley): Asked about AI investment and whether 2026 is a high point for spending. Wasserman responded that investment growth is “a sustainable long-term model,” with significant AI investment included under digital and technology.
  • Multiple analysts probed for details on loan growth, cost saves, deposit betas, and revenue synergies, with management consistently emphasizing momentum, but deferring some revenue synergy guidance to future updates.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly pressing, with many seeking clarity on expense layering, synergy realization timing, and baseline guidance figures, but no indications of deep skepticism or negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently reiterating “confidence” and emphasizing certainty and momentum; for example, Wasserman said, “Very, very confident” regarding the 55% efficiency ratio target.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained optimistic and focused on growth, with a slight increase in emphasis on integration execution and synergy realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance provided more explicit details on Cadence integration, including expected NII and fee revenue contributions, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on Veritex. The 2026 outlook now projects higher growth rates in NII, loans, deposits, and fees than previously experienced in 2025.
  • Management reiterated and expanded on its cost discipline and operating leverage focus, now targeting 150 to 200 basis points in 2026, following 290 basis points achieved in 2025.
  • Analyst questions shifted from general growth momentum and credit quality (Q3) to specifics of integration, expense layering, and timing of synergies (Q4).
  • Management’s confidence in achieving revenue and cost synergies from both partnerships was more pronounced, with a clearer timeline for realization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management recognized a competitive deposit environment but expressed confidence in “optimizing volume growth and pricing.”
  • The criticized asset ratio increased to 4.2%, primarily due to Veritex’s commercial real estate loans, but remained within historical range.
  • Integration risk was addressed, with management emphasizing rapid and collaborative decision-making to retain talent and minimize disruption.
  • Analysts raised concerns about expense run rates, timing of cost saves, and potential for additional M&A, all of which management addressed by reiterating organic growth focus and disciplined investment.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Huntington’s strong 2025 results set a foundation for even greater growth in 2026, backed by substantial investments in digital, rapid integration of strategic partnerships, and a disciplined approach to costs and capital. The company expects to realize both cost and revenue synergies from Veritex and Cadence, driving outperformance in net interest income, fee revenue, and operating leverage, while maintaining a moderate risk profile and expanding its presence in high-growth markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. to formally exit WHO with no decision on unpaid fees
data.description:
There is uncertainty over whether the Trump administration will settle hundreds of millions of dollars in fees it owes to the World Health Organization ((WHO)) as the U.S. is set to leave the global body officially on Thursday.

On his first day in office in January 2025, Trump issued an executive order to withdraw the U.S. from the WHO, triggering a budgetary crisis in the 194-member organization and prompting global health experts to urge the president to reconsider his decision.

“I hope the U.S. will reconsider and rejoin,” WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said during a press conference earlier this month, adding, “Withdrawing from the WHO is a loss for the United States, and it’s a loss for the rest of the world.”

Under U.S. law, the government is required to issue a one-year notice and settle all outstanding fees before the exit. According to National Public Radio, the country has yet to settle $278M in unpaid fees to the WHO for the 2024-2025 period.

The U.S. State Department has not clarified whether the U.S. could exit the WHO without paying its dues. On Tuesday, the agency told NPR, "The United States will not be making any payments to the WHO before our withdrawal."

"The cost borne by the U.S. taxpayer and U.S. economy after the WHO's failure during the COVID pandemic—and since—has been too high as it is," it added.

data.title: AI will be undersupplied by memory in the upcoming years – Cantor Fitzgerald
data.description:
Artificial intelligence will face a severe memory shortage throughout 2026 and 2027, according to CJ Muse, senior analyst at Cantor Fitzgerald.

The shortage is expected to create “tremendous earnings power” for memory chip manufacturers, with stocks like Sandisk (SNDK) and Micron Technology (MU) positioned for significant gains.

During an interview with CNBC, Muse said this memory cycle is fundamentally different from previous ones.

“From a NAND (a non-volatile storage technology that retains data without a power source) perspective, we’ve been in essentially a seven-year down cycle,” Muse explained. “And so, there’s no urgency from any of the NAND players to add capacity. And when you layer in the incremental growth from AI, I think we’re going to be undersupplied for all of '26 and '27.”

The analyst pointed to potentially massive earnings beats ahead, noting that Sandisk (SNDK) could earn $10 to $12 per share in the March quarter, far exceeding the Street’s estimate of approximately $4.

“When you think about that seismic beat in the world of semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX), (XSD), I hearken back to Micron (MU) in December and NVIDIA (NVDA) three years ago for that kind of material beat to numbers,” Muse said.

The memory shortage will also directly impact everyday consumers, according to Muse. He projected that PC unit volumes will decline 10% this year while smartphone volumes will fall at least 5%.

“At the high end, higher prices. And at the low end, you’ll see despecing of memory,” he warned.

Supply constraints are acute, with Muse citing a recent dinner where Micron (MU) discussed being “unable to provide 30 to 50%” of what certain large customers requested. Building a new greenfield fab takes more than two years, meaning efforts by Samsung (SSNLF) and SK Hynix (HXSC.F) to add capacity won’t alleviate shortages until early 2027 at the earliest.

Given the added demand from high bandwidth memory and agentic-led AI servers, Muse does not see the shortage being fully resolved until 2028.

“This is definitely a cycle unlike any other cycle I’ve seen,” he concluded. “I do think that peak multiples will be higher this time, and I think peak earnings will not be '26 but '27, and that means there’s still upside.”

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Inspire downgraded at Oppenheimer following CMS NCD correction
data.description:
• Inspire Medical Systems (INSP) was downgraded by Oppenheimer to perform from outperform after CMS issued a correction that obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is not a covered indication under the vagus nerve stimulation National Coverage Determination.
  • As a result, analyst Suraj Kalia said that "inventory sales/new site dynamics will now be pressured, FY26 guidance might need recalibration."
  • Shares are down 12% in Thursday afternoon trading.
  • Inspire manufactures an implantable device that uses neurostimulation technology to treat OSA.
  • Kalia predicts a price elasticity of demand shift for the implants as well.
  • He added that while Inspire may have been an acquisition target, the "timing/rationale/economics of such a move could become a head scratcher."

data.title: Great Lakes Dredge, Orion soar to multiyear highs as J.P. Morgan initiates with buy ratings
data.description:
Great Lakes Dredge & Dock (GLDD) +8% in Thursday's trading to its highest in more than four years as J.P. Morgan initiated coverage with an Overweight rating and year-end $20 price target, saying the largest U.S. dredging contractor is "entering a multi-year upcycle driven by record government infrastructure spending, a $1B-plus backlog, and expansion into offshore energy."

The company's strong backlog provides strong revenue visibility into 2026, while a shift toward higher-margin capital projects and disciplined cost management is enhancing profitability, and fleet upgrades should further support margin expansion, JPM analyst Tomohiko Sano said.

Great Lakes' (GLDD) entry into offshore energy "creates a new growth vertical, positioning
it to benefit from the global energy transition," Sano wrote, expecting double-digit EPS growth ahead and viewing the stock's risk/reward as attractive given robust demand and operational momentum.

Orion Group (ORN) +14.8% after JPM initiated the marine services company at Overweight with a $16 PT, calling it "a pure-play beneficiary of historical U.S. infrastructure and data center construction cycles."

Orion's (ORN) "macro exposure, and its improving operational trajectory under new leadership, will support sustained revenue growth and margin expansion," Sano said.

data.title: Ford Credit Bank, GM Financial Bank applications approved by FDIC
data.description:
The board of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp., or FDIC, approved deposit insurance applications submitted by Ford Motor (F) to establish Ford Credit Bank and by General Motors (GM) to establish GM Financial Bank, the agency said Thursday.

Both will be Utah-chartered industrial banks.

Ford Credit Bank's and GM Financial Bank's proposed business models will focus on providing automotive financing products nationwide. Ford Credit intends to purchase retail installment sales contracts from independent Ford dealers. GM Financial Bank's plans to purchase retail installment sales contracts from GMF.

In both cases, funding will primarily consist of retail savings accounts and time deposits obtained through the banks' websites and mobile apps, the FDIC said.

They'll both be required to maintain a minimum 15% tier 1 leverage ratio. Each parent company will be required to support its respective bank's capital and liquidity positions.

data.title: Southern Missouri Bancorp signals mid-single-digit loan growth target for fiscal 2026 while expanding repurchase program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Southern Missouri Bancorp (SMBC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Matthew Funke, President & Chief Administrative Officer, reported, "Quarter-over-quarter, our earnings and profitability improved due to a lower provision for credit losses, a larger earning asset base, which drove an increase in net interest income as well as an increase in noninterest income." Funke stated that diluted EPS for the quarter was $1.62, representing an increase of $0.24 from the linked September quarter. He detailed that gross loan balances increased by $35 million during the quarter, with "growth led by 1-4 family residential, C&I and construction and loan development loans." Funke highlighted "almost $312 million" in loan originations, the strongest in several years, with a loan pipeline of $159 million at quarter end. He expressed confidence about achieving "mid-single-digit growth for the fiscal year of '26."

  • The company repurchased 148,000 shares for a total of $8.1 million in the quarter, with an average purchase price "122% of our tangible book value as of December 31, '25."

  • Greg Steffens, Chairman & CEO, explained that "overall problem asset levels have increased slightly since last quarter but remain at modest levels," noting adversely classified loans of $59 million, up $4 million since last quarter. Steffens described non-performing loans of about $30 million and highlighted nonaccruals attributed to two borrowing relationships: one in commercial real estate and equipment and another in agricultural production loans. He emphasized, "Importantly, we made meaningful progress on the specialty CRE relationship we have discussed in prior quarters, meaningfully reducing our exposure and the resulting net recovery for the quarter."

  • Stefan Chkautovich, Executive VP, CFO & Principal Financial Officer, stated, "This quarter's net interest margin of 3.57% was flat compared to the linked September quarter." He added, "Looking ahead to the March quarter, declining interest rates are beginning to pressure loan yields as loans mature... That said, we continue to see an opportunity for further improvement in funding costs."

  • Steffens announced, "With the near completion of our prior share repurchase authorization, our Board approved a new program to repurchase up to 550,000 shares or approximately 5% of shares outstanding." He also noted, "Since last quarter, we have continued M&A discussions as market conditions have stabilized and general M&A activity has picked up in the industry."

Outlook

  • Funke commented that, given seasonal loan trends, "we would expect limited net loan growth in the March quarter, but having grown just above 3% in our fiscal year-to-date and expecting a typical pickup of growth in our fourth quarter, we're still in a good position to achieve mid-single-digit growth for the fiscal year of '26."
  • Chkautovich stated, "We do still see potential for increased spread to pick up in the March quarter due to decrease in deposit costs."
  • Regarding expenses, Chkautovich explained, "we would expect to see a quarterly increase in the compensation expense run rate in the March quarter as annual merit increases and cost of living adjustments take effect... including the cost of benefits."

Financial Results

  • Funke reported diluted EPS of $1.62 for the December quarter, an increase of $0.24 from the prior quarter and $0.32 from the year-ago quarter.
  • Provision for credit loss expense was $1.7 million, down $2.8 million from the September quarter.
  • Gross loan balances rose by $35 million in the quarter, and by almost $200 million year-over-year.
  • Deposit balances increased by $28 million in the quarter, and by $98 million compared to the prior year.
  • Net interest margin was 3.57%, flat quarter-over-quarter, and up from 3.34% a year ago.
  • Tangible book value per share was $44.65, up $5.74 over 12 months.
  • The company reported net recoveries of 7 basis points annualized, compared to net charge-offs of 36 basis points in the linked quarter.
  • Non-interest income increased 3.1% quarter-over-quarter.

Q&A

  • Matt Olney, Stephens Inc.: Asked about unexpected loan paydowns and competition. Steffens replied, "we had several unexpected paydowns that we were not really fully anticipating, but we weren't disappointed to see several of those... loan prepayment rates have been higher than what we've historically seen."
  • On loan pricing competition, Funke said, "definitely, there still is some aggressive competition out there."
  • Olney asked about net interest margin outlook. Chkautovich responded, "we don't give specific guidance on the NIM. But underlying, we do still see potential for increased spread to pick up in the March quarter due to decrease in deposit costs."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Queried about personnel cost increases. Chkautovich answered, "this is just a seasonal adjustment for annual merit increases. So that's in the ballpark of mid-single-digit increase there."
  • On buyback activity, Funke noted, "we're going to be somewhat price dependent, thinking about how useful it is to deploy the capital there versus retaining it, waiting for a better opportunity."
  • Race asked about charge-offs on the CRE loan. Steffens replied, "We would anticipate being more to historical averages over the upcoming quarters would not anticipate any much of the way of a tailwind behind us."
  • Charles Driscoll, KBW: Asked about deposit betas. Chkautovich stated, "we've seen betas around the 40% level. That would probably be something good to use for modeling purposes."
  • On M&A preferences, Steffens said, "We would prefer M&A within our footprint, but if something is right adjacent to us, I mean, that's something we definitely would look at."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on loan paydowns, margin outlook, and expense guidance, with a neutral and factual tone throughout the session.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Steffens stating, "we are very pleased with the level and quality of our earnings this quarter." In Q&A, management remained open and factual, occasionally noting, "we do still feel good about our mid-single-digit loan growth projections."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed a consistent, steady tone, though management expressed slightly more optimism regarding credit workout progress and capital flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for mid-single-digit loan growth for fiscal 2026 was reaffirmed, with management noting strong loan originations in the December quarter but expecting seasonal moderation in the March quarter.
  • The provision for credit loss decreased significantly from the previous quarter, while nonperforming assets and classified loans increased modestly, largely attributed to a few specific relationships.
  • Share repurchase activity increased sharply from just over 8,000 shares to 148,000 shares quarter-over-quarter, with a new program authorizing up to 550,000 shares.
  • Management tone shifted slightly more positive on progress in working out specialty CRE credits and on capital deployment options, while analysts’ questions remained focused on asset quality and margin sustainability.
  • Strategic focus continues on disciplined capital deployment, M&A opportunities, and managing credit risk, with management reporting increased M&A discussions as market activity picks up.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged increases in nonperforming loans and classified assets, primarily from a small number of relationships, but emphasized, "overall problem assets remain at manageable levels and our earnings are sufficient to cover potential reserves while maintaining above-average profitability."
  • Steffens noted, "we are not complacent with recent trends and remain focused on improving credit quality."
  • Concerns for agricultural lending persist, with lower commodity prices and higher production costs expected to create operating shortfalls for some farm customers, though management stated, "we anticipate that our borrowers will generally be able to navigate another challenging year."

Final Takeaway

Southern Missouri Bancorp’s management highlighted improved profitability, a reduction in credit loss provisions, and record loan originations for the quarter while reaffirming mid-single-digit loan growth targets for fiscal 2026. The board authorized a substantial new share repurchase program, and management pointed to an active M&A pipeline backed by strong capital levels. Asset quality metrics saw modest pressure from a handful of borrower relationships, but management remains confident in credit workout strategies and disciplined capital deployment to drive shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Downtown Las Vegas casino owner gets creative in trying to convince Canadians to return
data.description:
Casino owner Derek Stevens has a plan to lure Canadians back to Las Vegas following a weak year of tourism trends from the nation that typically sends the most traffic to the gambling mecca.

Stevens, who owns the Golden Gate Hotel & Casino, The D Las Vegas, and Circa Resort & Casino in downtown Las Vegas, said his properties will accept Canadian dollars on a par with the U.S. dollar, which will give Canadians a significant discount at the current exchange rate of $1.00 U.S. dollar to $0.73 Canadian dollar. The promotion will run until August 31 and applies to hotel rooms, bars at the three properties, and $500 worth of casino play. Notably, Circa Resorts & Casino opened in October 2020 as the first new hotel-casino built from the ground up in downtown Las Vegas in roughly four decades, and it is positioned as a modern homage to classic Las Vegas history, as well as a value play for tourists in comparison to Strip properties.

Underpinning the appeal to the Great White North, Canadian tourism to Las Vegas has slumped sharply over the past year. The Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority estimated Canadian visitation fell close to 25% in 2024, while overall tourism to Las Vegas was down about 7%. Airline seat capacity from Canadian cities to Las Vegas has declined around 40% to the lowest level since about 2006. The biggest factor cited in recent surveys was resentment against U.S. policies toward Canada. Other factors included value perception of Las Vegas trips, the exchange rate, and travel expenses in general.

MGM Resorts (MGM) and Caesars Entertainment (CZR) are the two biggest casino operators in Las Vegas.

data.title: Ted Sarandos to testify at a February Senate hearing on the Netflix-Warner deal
data.description:
Netflix Inc. (NFLX) co-chief executive Ted Sarandos is planning to testify in February in front of a U.S. Senate committee that is scrutinizing the company's $82.7B deal for the streaming and studio assets of Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (WBD), Bloomberg reported on Thursday.

Bruce Campbell, the strategy chief at Warner Bros., is also planning to appear, sources told Bloomberg. Executives from Paramount Skydance Corp. (PSKY) aren’t expected to testify at the hearing.

While Netflix is expected to win the bidding war for Warner's assets, its revised $27.75 per share all-cash offer is considered more complicated from an antitrust perspective due to the streaming giant’s dominant position in subscription video-on-demand across Europe.

Paramount, with strong ties to the current Trump administration, is not backing down and is relentlessly pursuing the HBO parent's coveted assets through a $30 per share hostile takeover bid.

Senior executives from the David Ellison-run media company have held talks with French President Emmanuel Macron and discussed with UK officials as part of a European charm offensive to gain support for their offer.

Even U.S. President Donald Trump recently voiced his support for a Paramount-Warner Bros. deal on his social media platform.

While Hollywood critics have expressed concerns about the impact of a Netflix-WBD deal, Sarandos, in an extensive interview published in The New York Times last week, reassured that the theatrical industry will not be impacted.
